title: The Death of God
wikitext:
[]: The Death of God             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Sat Nov 24, 2007 9:07 pm I just found something that might be up the Imperialist's alley: I found it as a part of a reference to an Akira Kurosawa movie named Ran, meaning "revolt","war" or "chaos", and it's pretty interesting. Kurosawa himself said, "Humanity must face life without relying on God or Buddha.", which is referenced to almost directly in Keel's line "We shall not rely on the power of Adam or the Angels". In addition, there's a theme called "Death of God", in which God is considered dead not in the physical sense, but that the moral and theistic concepts of a God cannot be relied upon the keep the world in line, and humans do not believe in any cosmic order since they don't recognize one. It's a very interesting theme. http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/God_is_dead http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ran_%28film%29 Without Adam or the Angels or any godlike force, Seele decides to make humanity a sort of superhuman entity, the "Uebermensch" that Nietzsche speaks about. Whether or not Anno got his ideas from here, the themes are remarkably similar in my opinion. Do read through, and tell me what you think? (I posted this in a new thread because the old Nietzsche threads are in shambles)

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Nov 25, 2007 2:42 am Within NGE the term "God" is abused liberally. To the point where it seems to mean vastly different things. Adam was "God" because he had the power to create life (God as in the creator). Instrumentality was the "path to godhood" because it would turn all Lilim into a perfect entity (God as in perfection). There's a lot of very interesting themes along these lines, and I think NGE perhaps purposefully obscures the idea of what "God" means and is. Under my interpretation, it more often than not is used as a concept rather than an actual being. Oh, and someone will undoubtedly mention "They were just using it to be cool." (I call BS).

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Sun Nov 25, 2007 2:58 am I thought it came across rather clear as meaning beings greater than ourselves for whatever reasons.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Nov 25, 2007 5:09 am That's rather obscure though. I mean, would we be Gods to humans from 100 years ago? I just take it to mean what I said; various concepts on what God entails - omnipotent (Adam), omniscient (Yui), omnipresent (Rei/Lilith).

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Sun Nov 25, 2007 5:43 am They have found god, but they faced some damnation, and they want to rebuild a God for themselves. They can’t face the reality that God is Dead (well, at the least what Nietzsche wanted to say), and they had this half-botched attempt at humanity rising to become God. It shows that humans need a god for their peace of mind and soul, and because they face the reality of losing one, they, not wanting to lose that ‘peace’ wants to become God Himself. As Nietzsche said “God is what man desires to be, the perfect image of themselves” Thank you for bringing this up.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Nov 25, 2007 6:00 am The idea of God as a supreme deity to be worshiped is never really discussed in NGE, so I really don't think the "God is dead" theme really fits with how Nietzsche intended it. Seele (specifically) within NGE are not looking towards God for a basis in moral righteousness, but rather as a state to be attained that will solve the inherent flaws in humanity. God is Dead seems aimed more towards the uprooting of long held beliefs (mostly Christian) which claim the basis for their morality (God) is universal, objective, and absolute. And with the acceptance of "God is dead" came an acceptance of the subjectivity of moral beliefs. I really don't see that theme in NGE (but that might just be me).

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Sun Nov 25, 2007 6:03 am They are using metaphors, as usual, I think.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Sun Nov 25, 2007 6:17 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: I mean, would we be Gods to humans from 100 years ago?

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Sun Nov 25, 2007 7:35 am Seele were trying to use Adam to their own purposes, and later his human incarnate Kaworu. What they were up to is never specified, and what happened at 2I still has a healthy aura of mystery about it. They might have been trying to use the nearly Godlike Adam, a messenger of the unseen Gods, to further their own purposes. And then come the Evas. Seele clearly says, "We did not want to create a new God" when it came to Eva-01 assimilating Zeruel's S2 engine. Eva-01 being a "Bunshin of Lilith" might have made her a God where the S2-equipped Mass Production Evangelions and Angels were not. Evas, of course, are a creation of the Lilim, a copy, an imitation, one that is limited in its power one way or the other, but which still serves as a substitute for a God-figure, a potential Uebermensh the way they are. In the end, it is neither the two progenitor divinities nor their S2 equipped offspring that make the final decision. Instead of a perfect divinity, an ordinary human teenager takes the final decision on the future of the human race. Men have to solve their own problems where God doesn't hold. Of course, Nietzsche's theme was much more into society like already stated, but the fundamentals of his theme(or a semblance of) are applicable.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Sun Nov 25, 2007 7:54 am UrsusArctos wrote: Eva-01 being a "Bunshin of Lilith" might have made her a God where the S2-equipped Mass Production Evangelions and Angels were not.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Sun Nov 25, 2007 8:08 am "Kami" means divinity, and that fits with the rather loose definition of a God in the show... All right, another divinity wasn't needed. But why do the Harpies have S2s? And there of course is the Lilith no Bunshin issue, and what Keel and Teddy say when Kaworu hijacks Nigoki. Something is seriously wrong with having Adam or Angels about, and Seele agrees that they've blundered enough. Keel is happy with what happens in EoE. Does that mean that he's managed to get what he wanted, without being overly dependent on a previously existent entity he had no control of?

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Sun Nov 25, 2007 8:18 am Don't look a gift horse in the mouth. If Lilith is going to give you what you want don't complain about not having strings to pull. Of corse it's always better to have those lovely strings if at all possible so you know you're getting what you want beyond a shadow of a doubt. In the end they got cheated by Lilith because she gave the choice to Shinji proving their fears correct, go figure...

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Nov 25, 2007 9:30 am Ursus, I'm not quite sure what you're on about. I read what you're saying, but I guess I'm missing the point (or the question). The Imperialist wrote: They are using metaphors, as usual, I think.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Sun Nov 25, 2007 10:04 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: So, in a way, I can see those three falling under those categories. But, you might, perhaps, have to stretch it a bit.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Nov 25, 2007 10:20 am Anonymous_Evafan wrote: Lilith falls far closer to true omnipotence with her indifference to temporal mechanics, ability to project her soul in billions of places at once.

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Sun Nov 25, 2007 11:07 am The Death of God thing could be applied here as in the end, people will eventually let go for their obsession with God (or in literal sense right here, let go with their obsession for themselves be integrated with each other, becoming God themselves- interesting paradox, coming into conflict with Nietzsche’s ideals of the Superman)

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Nov 25, 2007 11:17 am One interesting theme I've been rolling around in my head lately I got from NGE's connection to 2001. That's the idea that mankind has defined itself by its creations/tools. Both NGE and 2001 deal with the limitations of this paradigm. In 2001 we have the cross cut of the bone becoming a nuclear reactor, and in NGE we have the idea that all the weapons of mass destruction cannot defeat the Angels because of their "AT Field" (the light of the soul). So both seem to be saying that we're trying to reach godhood through science and technology, but that ultimately this paradigm is a dead end. Both seem to portray that the next step in evolution must come from within, rather than from without. An this evolution will act as a kind of rebirth. Anyway, how that relates to this idea is simply that mankind seems to be trying to reach God/perfection through our creations, and ultimately, they have become our juvenile representation of God. "The Death of God" could in a metaphorical sense be equated with abandoning this paradigm we've latched onto as the defining element of our existence.

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Sun Nov 25, 2007 12:16 pm Jawohl.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sun Nov 25, 2007 12:22 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: In 2001 we have the cross cut of the bone becoming a nuclear reactor

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Sun Nov 25, 2007 12:28 pm Interesting... but hey, the possibility is endless. Until the book shuts, the author will not open its mouth (opposite of Nietzsche- "When the book opens its mouth, the author shuts its mouth")
